prompt: Recent demonstrations (>200 qubits) of neutral-atom quantum processors using optical-tweezer arrays
article:
# Neutral-atom optical-tweezer quantum processors above 200 qubits since 2020

## Executive summary

Neutral-atom processors based on optical tweezer arrays have expanded from **hundreds of programmable, strongly interacting Rydberg-atom qubits (2020ŌĆō2022)** to demonstrations that separately push **(i) fault-tolerant digital architectures at the few-hundred-qubit scale** and **(ii) ultra-large, highly coherent storage-and-transport registers at the multiŌĆæthousandŌĆæqubit scale**. The strongest, primary-source-supported milestones since 2020 are:

* **256 programmable Rydberg-array platform (Harvard/Lukin et al., Nature, 2021):** deterministic preparation of **defect-free 2D arrays up to 256 atoms**, using an SLM-generated static tweezer grid plus AOD-based moving tweezers for rearrangement, and coherent coupling to a **Rydberg state with principal quantum number n = 70**. Key metrics include rearrangement to **>99% filling** for a few hundred atoms and a **~400 ms experimental cycle time** for >200-site arrays in that implementation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn11view0Ņłü  
* **289-qubit optimization (Harvard/QuEra/MIT et al., Science, 2022):** experimental study of **maximum independent set** (MIS) optimization using **Rydberg blockade** on **up to 289 qubits in 2D**, implementing QAOA-like and quasi-adiabatic pulse programs and benchmarking against classical simulated annealing. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn7view0Ņłü  
* **Commercially exposed ŌĆ£FPQAŌĆØ analog system (QuEra Aquila 1.0 whitepaper, arXiv, 2023):** a public-facing neutral-atom tweezer processor operating as an **analog Hamiltonian simulator** on **up to 256 neutral-atom qubits**, describing operational constraints (e.g., **minimum site spacing 4 ╬╝m**, about **75 ╬╝m ├Ś 76 ╬╝m placement area**) and a **shot rate <10 Hz** due to destructive readout. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn14view0Ņłü  
* **Thousand-scale trapping and rearrangement at cryogenic temperature (Pasqal/Institut dŌĆÖOptique et al., arXiv, 2024):** trapping of **single ŌüĖŌüĘRb atoms** in arrays up to **2088 sites** at **6 K**, including **1103 trapped atoms** in a single-shot example and **atom-by-atom rearrangement of a 828-trap target** (Ōēł95% occupancy reported) using moving tweezers controlled by an FPGA. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn15view0Ņłéturn5search22Ņłü  
* **Logical (encoded) computation on a 256ŌĆæqubit Yb tweezer processor (Atom Computing + Microsoft et al., arXiv, 2024):** demonstration of **logical-qubit entanglement (24 logical qubits)** and **BernsteinŌĆōVazirani** with **up to 28 logical qubits encoded into 112 physical atoms**, on a **256ŌĆæqubit ┬╣ŌüĘ┬╣Yb** neutral-atom processor with **all-to-all connectivity enabled by atom movement**. Reported physical-layer benchmarks include **1Q Clifford fidelity 99.85(2)%** and **two-qubit CZ gate infidelity as low as 0.35(5)% (echoed RB without moves)** (post-selected on remaining in the qubit subspace). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü  
* **6100 highly coherent atomic qubits in ~12,000 tweezers (Caltech/Endres lab, Nature Accelerated Article Preview, accepted 2025):** **>6100 ┬╣┬│┬│Cs hyperfine qubits** in **11,998 sites**, with **12.6(1) s coherence**, **~23 min trapping lifetime**, **imaging survival 99.98952(1)%** and **imaging fidelity 99.99374(8)%**, plus **single-qubit gate fidelity 99.9834(2)%** and coherence-preserving transport operations measured via (interleaved) randomized benchmarking. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü  
* **Up to 448 atoms in a fault-tolerant neutral-atom architecture (Harvard/Lukin + QuEra, Nature, published online 10 Nov 2025; issue Jan 2026):** a reconfigurable multi-zone architecture with **ŌüĖŌüĘRb clock-state qubits in 852ŌĆænm tweezers**, **Raman single-qubit control**, **Rydberg-mediated 2Q gates to n = 53** with a reported **CZ gate fidelity improving from 99.3% to 99.5%** with added spacing between gates, and **mid-circuit qubit reuse** enabling **fast cycle times (example 4 ms)** and deep, error-corrected logical protocols. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn23view0Ņłéturn34view0Ņłü  

A key analytical takeaway is that **ŌĆ£>200 qubitsŌĆØ now spans two partially decoupled frontiers**: (1) **hundreds of qubits with programmable two-qubit gates, mid-circuit measurement, and repeated QEC** (e.g., up to 448 atoms in the Harvard/QuEra FTQC architecture), and (2) **thousands of qubits with exceptional coherence/imaging/transport** but **not yet** demonstrated at the same scale with dense, programmable entangling-gate fabrics (e.g., 6100 Cs qubits focused on coherence, imaging survival, and shuttling primitives). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn23view0Ņłéturn17view0Ņłü  

## Scope and methodology

This report is limited to **neutral-atom quantum processors using optical tweezer arrays** with **>200 qubits/atoms demonstrated** in **public primary sources since 2020** (peer-reviewed papers, arXiv preprints, and official company announcements). When a source does not specify a requested parameter (e.g., blockade radius), it is explicitly marked **unspecified**. When a value is computed from stated parameters, it is labeled as **inferred** (not directly claimed by the source).  

Primary sources used include: Nature (2021, 2025/2026), Science (2022 paper copy hosted by MIT), arXiv preprints (2024), and official vendor materials (QuEra arXiv whitepaper; Pasqal newsroom; Atom Computing press release and whitepaper). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn7view0Ņłéturn14view0Ņłéturn15view0Ņłéturn33view0Ņłéturn17view0Ņłéturn23view0Ņłéturn5search22Ņłéturn20view0Ņłéturn21view0Ņłü  

## Timeline of >200-qubit tweezer demonstrations since 2020

```mermaid
timeline
    title Neutral-atom optical-tweezer processors >200 qubits (2020ŌĆō2026)
    2021-07-07 : Ebadi et al. (Nature) programmable 2D Rydberg simulator up to 256 87Rb atoms; SLM + moving AOD rearrangement; Rydberg n=70
    2022-06-10 : Ebadi et al. (Science) MIS optimization with up to 289 qubits in 2D; QAOA-like and quasi-adiabatic pulse programs
    2023-06 : QuEra "Aquila 1.0" (arXiv whitepaper) analog FPQA on up to 256 Rb-87 qubits; <10 Hz shot rate; min site spacing 4 ╬╝m
    2024-05-31 : Pichard et al. (arXiv) 2088-site cryogenic (6 K) 87Rb tweezer array; 828-atom target rearrangement (~95% occupancy)
    2024-11-18 : Reichardt et al. (arXiv) logical computation on a 256-qubit 171Yb tweezer processor; 24 logical-qubit cat; BV up to 28 logical qubits
    2025-09-17 : Manetsch et al. (Nature accepted) 11,998 tweezers trapping >6,100 Cs-133 qubits; 12.6 s coherence; 99.99%+ imaging metrics; coherent transport RB
    2025-11-10 : Bluvstein et al. (Nature) FTQC architecture in 87Rb tweezers up to 448 atoms; Rydberg n=53; mid-circuit reuse; deep logical teleportation protocols
```

(For the Caltech ŌĆ£6100 qubitsŌĆØ paper, the accessible Nature ŌĆ£Accelerated Article PreviewŌĆØ PDF records **Received 20 March 2024** and **Accepted 17 September 2025**, but the preview header itself does not yet show the final ŌĆ£Published onlineŌĆØ date; the Nature DOI is provided in that PDF. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü)

## Cross-cutting technical analysis

### Species and qubit encodings

A clear architectural split appears in the sources:

Harvard/QuEraŌĆÖs earlier largeŌĆæN demonstrations (2021ŌĆō2022) use an **effective spinŌĆæ1/2** encoded directly in the **ground state |gŌ¤® and a Rydberg state |rŌ¤®** of **ŌüĖŌüĘRb**, driven globally with tunable ╬®(t), ╬ö(t), and interactions for analog simulation and constrained optimization. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn7view0Ņłü This choice naturally supports blockade-based constraints and analog Ising-type dynamics, but does not present a conventional gate set in those papers.

Later ŌĆ£fault-tolerant architectureŌĆØ work from the same ecosystem (Nature 2025/2026) uses **hyperfine clock states of ŌüĖŌüĘRb** (mF = 0) for storage and single-qubit gates (Raman), while still using **Rydberg excitation (n = 53)** only during entangling operations to limit exposure to Rydberg-specific decoherence and to enable mid-circuit readout/reuse. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn26view0Ņłéturn29view0Ņłü

Atom ComputingŌĆÖs logical-computation paper uses **┬╣ŌüĘ┬╣Yb nuclear-spin qubits in the ┬╣SŌéĆ manifold (mF = ┬▒1/2)**, with Raman single-qubit operations and a **Rydberg-mediated CZ**, exploiting a design in which certain errors are converted into **detectable atom loss (ŌĆ£erasure conversionŌĆØ)**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü This encoding is attractive for long-lived memory and low sensitivity to magnetic noise (the paper notes they do not automatically apply dynamical decoupling because the nuclear-spin qubit is ŌĆ£well-isolatedŌĆØ), though the paper does not report a single headline TŌéé in its text. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü

Caltech/EndresŌĆÖs 6100-qubit paper emphasizes **┬╣┬│┬│Cs hyperfine qubits** with extremely long coherence and record imaging survival, explicitly choosing **far-off-resonant nearŌĆæIR trapping** (1055/1061 nm) and leveraging CsŌĆÖs high polarizability at those wavelengths to scale to high trap counts. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü

### Tweezer generation, geometry, and reconfigurability

Across these sources, the dominant pattern is **static trap generation + dynamic rearrangement**, but with different scaling strategies.

In the Harvard 256ŌĆæatom Nature 2021 platform, a **static SLM** produces a large 2D tweezer field (up to ~1000 tweezers loaded probabilistically), while **moving AOD tweezers** deterministically rearrange atoms into defect-free patterns; rearrangement is reported to take **50ŌĆō100 ms** for up to a few hundred atoms in that implementation. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn11view0Ņłü

QuEraŌĆÖs Aquila whitepaper describes similar SLM + AOD mechanics and further documents **geometry constraints** relevant to public programming on that system (e.g., **no two qubits within 4 ╬╝m**, and a bounded placement aperture). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn14view0Ņłü

PasqalŌĆÖs 2024 cryogenic demonstration focuses on scaling site count (Ōēł2000 tweezers) and shows deterministic rearrangement to **828** targets using moving tweezers controlled by an FPGA, while reporting that **finite lifetime (~100 s)** plus rearrangement imperfections limit final occupancy (~95% for the 828-target example). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn15view0Ņłéturn5search22Ņłü

EndresŌĆÖs 6100-qubit work scales by combining (i) **very high optical power** (130 W from fiber amplifiers; 35ŌĆō40 W at the objective) and (ii) a large field of view (1.5 mm diameter) with **~12k traps spaced ~7.2 ╬╝m**, achieving uniform loading and high-fidelity imaging at unprecedented atom number. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü

The Harvard/QuEra fault-tolerant architecture is especially notable for formalizing **zoned layouts** (storage / entangling / readout / reservoir), where **AOD-driven movement** is not only a loading tool but also a **mid-circuit routing primitive** to realize arbitrary connectivity and to localize Rydberg exposure. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn23view0Ņłéturn29view0Ņłü

### Gate sets, native operations, and how ŌĆ£>200 qubitsŌĆØ is used

A rigorous comparison requires separating three regimes:

Analog, globally driven Rydberg dynamics (simulation/optimization). The 2021 Nature 256ŌĆæatom paper and the 2022 MIS Science paper implement programmable many-body evolution primarily through **global Rydberg excitation plus long-range interactions**, rather than compiling algorithms into an explicit 1Q/2Q gate set. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn7view0Ņłü In this regime, ŌĆ£benchmarkingŌĆØ typically focuses on state preparation probabilities, correlation functions, and application-level performance (e.g., approximation ratio / success probabilities in MIS), rather than RB-style gate errors.

Digital architectures with movement-enabled connectivity. Atom ComputingŌĆÖs arXiv 2024 work explicitly describes a **register + interaction zone** architecture where up to 8 pairs perform Rydberg-mediated **CZ** in parallel and movement gives **all-to-all connectivity**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü This is more directly comparable to conventional circuit models.

Fault-tolerant logical processing (mid-circuit measurement + QEC). The Harvard/QuEra Nature 2025/2026 architecture uses **surface codes**, **transversal gates**, **lattice surgery**, and **teleportation-based techniques** and reports cycle-time data and gate-fidelity sensitivity relevant to scaling repeated QEC rounds and logical teleportations. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn23view0Ņłéturn34view6Ņłü

A key practical insight: todayŌĆÖs highest-qubit-count papers can be ŌĆ£processor-gradeŌĆØ but still differ strongly in how many of those qubits are simultaneously participating in **entangling-gate fabrics**. For example, the Harvard/QuEra FTQC paper describes entangling gates performed simultaneously on **up to 256 qubits** in a specific entangling-zone configuration, even though the full system is ŌĆ£up to 448 atoms.ŌĆØ ŅłĆciteŅłéturn24view0Ņłéturn23view0Ņłü Likewise, Atom ComputingŌĆÖs logical computation is executed on a 256-qubit processor but makes heavy use of subsets dedicated to code blocks and ancillas, consistent with the overheads of early logical-qubit experiments. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn31view0Ņłéturn33view0Ņłü

### Fidelities, coherence, and dominant error sources

The most directly comparable fidelity metrics in the >200-qubit regime come from the two gate-based papers (Harvard/QuEra FTQC; Atom Computing logical computation), plus the single-qubit RB in the 6100-qubit Cs paper.

Atom Computing (arXiv 2024) reports **1Q Clifford fidelity 99.85(2)%** and multiple 2Q CZ benchmarks, including **0.56(15)% CZ infidelity** (static IRB) and **0.35(5)% perŌĆæCZ error** in an echoed RB protocol without moves (post-selected on remaining in the qubit subspace), explicitly quantifying additional ŌĆ£loss + leakageŌĆØ channels. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü It also reports typical **atom loss probabilities per move below 0.1%**, important because movement is central to connectivity. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü

Harvard/QuEraŌĆÖs FTQC architecture highlights Rydberg-leakage-related issues and mitigation. In particular, it reports that in a repeated benchmarking sequence the **CZ fidelity increases from 99.3% to 99.5%** when increasing the duration between gates to **100 ╬╝s**, allowing problematic Rydberg population to decay/eject; it also notes that in motion-based circuits, between-gate times can be long enough (e.g., **400 ╬╝s** in surface-code stabilizer sequences) that this issue is ŌĆ£nativelyŌĆØ mitigated. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn24view6Ņłéturn34view7Ņłü The Methods also explicitly surface engineering concerns like AOD intermodulation and trap-depth inhomogeneity affecting coherence (including reduced T1 in some configurations). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn29view0Ņłéturn24view7Ņłü

Caltech/EndresŌĆÖs 6100-qubit work reports an exceptionally strong set of ŌĆ£usefulness metricsŌĆØ: **12.6(1) s hyperfine coherence**, **22.9(1) min lifetime**, **99.99%+ imaging survival/fidelity**, and **99.9834(2)% single-qubit gate fidelity**. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü It also identifies a practical dephasing contributor at scale: **dominant phase noise at 60 Hz** (mains), which becomes visible and must be engineered around in large arrays. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn19view1Ņłü

The FTQC Nature paper provides unusually concrete ŌĆ£system-improvementŌĆØ error budgeting: it states their current two-qubit error is ŌĆ£roughly 0.5%ŌĆØ and outlines laser-power and detuning changes that, by simulation-based estimates, could reduce scattering and dephasing terms and drive two-qubit error toward ~0.15% (with concomitant below-threshold margins). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn27view8Ņłü This is a rare example (in the >200ŌĆæqubit regime) of a published, parameter-level path from current entangling-gate errors to substantially improved values.

### Cooling and trapping methods

Cooling/trapping stacks are often the hidden determinant of run rate and usable qubit count:

Harvard/Lukin Nature 2021 loads from a MOT with **50ŌĆō60%** initial single-atom probability across up to ~1000 tweezers and then rearranges; it reports trap depths and motional parameters (waists, trap frequencies) and explicitly ties highest filling fractions to lifetime and duration of rearrangement. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn9view0Ņłéturn11view0Ņłü

Harvard/QuEra Nature 2025/2026 reports **D1 lambda-enhanced grey-molasses cooling** for improved loading efficiency (**75%**) into 852ŌĆænm SLM traps. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn26view0Ņłü It also uses specialized cooling/imaging in 1D-lattice-enabled mid-circuit readout workflows (detailed timings in Extended Data), underscoring that cooling is now interwoven with QEC schedules. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn34view6Ņłü

PasqalŌĆÖs cryogenic 6 K paper mixes cryogenic vacuum advantages with familiar MOT loading and polarization-gradient-cooling (PGC) approaches and quantifies trap properties (e.g., trap depth ~1.0 mK inferred from parametric heating; transverse trap frequency ~105 kHz). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn15view0Ņłü

Caltech/Endres uses parity-projection loading from a MOT, employs **2D polarization-gradient cooling (2D PGC)** during fluorescence imaging (80 ms), and introduces multi-step sequences to suppress false positives/background from unwanted trapsŌĆöprocedural details that matter at the 10kŌĆætweezer scale. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü

### Experimental run rates and the ŌĆ£classical control bottleneckŌĆØ

A striking recent shift is that once cycle times reach the fewŌĆæms regime, **classical compute and control** can dominate.

QuEra AquilaŌĆÖs publicly described operation emphasizes destructive readout and reloading, yielding **<10 Hz** shot rates. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn14view0Ņłü

By contrast, Harvard/QuEraŌĆÖs FTQC architecture explicitly demonstrates **4 ms cycle time** in a fast calibration example and provides a deep-circuit workflow breakdown where image processing and rearrangement waveforms executed on desktop computers set the pace (e.g., one logical teleportation layer **41.9 ms** in that implementation; Extended Data notes multi-ms conversion/imaging/cooling and ~7 ms latency for mid-circuit data processing). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn34view0Ņłéturn34view6Ņłü This supports a central practical conclusion: **hardware advances are now forcing a co-design between quantum control, classical real-time inference/decoding, and the experimentŌĆÖs data path**, not only the physics stack.

## Scalability claims, technical bottlenecks, and open challenges

### What the most recent sources claim about scaling

The Caltech/Endres 6100-qubit paper frames its results as showing that scaling to thousands of qubits can be compatible with high coherence and imaging performance, and it explicitly positions coherent transport and pick-up/drop-off as ingredients for **zone-based quantum computing** architectures. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü It also contextualizes these results against quantum error correction requirements (thousands of physical qubits at <10Ōü╗┬│ error rates for >100 logical qubits), using this to motivate why ŌĆ£thousandsŌĆØ is a qualitatively important milestone rather than just a record. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłü

The Harvard/QuEra FTQC architecture paper claims (and supports experimentally) that a carefully zoned, movement-enabled approach can deliver **repeated QEC below-threshold behavior** (in their studied circuit), ŌĆ£deep-circuit protocols with dozens of logical qubits,ŌĆØ and an operations model where teleportation and reuse reduce overheads. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn23view0Ņłéturn34view1Ņłü

### Persisting bottlenecks (as evidenced directly in the sources)

Atom loss and leakage remain first-class error channels in neutral atoms, but are increasingly exploited rather than merely tolerated. Atom Computing explicitly reports ŌĆ£erasure conversionŌĆØ (metastable-state population converts to loss in near tune-out traps) to make some gate faults detectable. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłü Harvard/QuEra similarly treats loss as structured information for decoders and architecture design; the paper describes loss-aware decoding and cancellation of subsequent gates after loss as part of the shot-specific error model. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn34view4Ņłü

Rydberg-state contamination / leakage and correlated error modes are explicitly called out. Harvard/QuEra note ŌĆ£leakage to Rydberg statesŌĆØ and reference ŌĆ£avalanche errorsŌĆØ known in dense Rydberg systems (while arguing their low density mitigates it), and they empirically probe detector-error distributions to look for burst events. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn24view12Ņłéturn34view4Ņłü This is crucial because such correlated errors can invalidate simple threshold assumptions and drive decoding difficulty.

Optical and RF control imperfections scale with array size. Caltech/Endres highlight mains-frequency noise (60 Hz) as a dominant phase noise source at scale. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn19view1Ņłü Harvard/QuEra list sensitivity to trap-depth inhomogeneity, beam-profile homogeneity (including a cited 2ŌĆō3% peak-to-peak inhomogeneity in a specific configuration), AOD intermodulation effects, and magnetic-field noise as practical limiters in deep-circuit contexts. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn29view0Ņłü

Classical-latency and waveform-streaming limits are now explicit experimental constraints. Harvard/QuEra state that AWG memory-limited waveform programming caps their demonstration at 27 layers in a deep-circuit protocol and emphasize that future improvements will benefit from better waveform streaming. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn26view0Ņłü

### Open challenges for >200 to >10,000 ŌĆ£computing-gradeŌĆØ qubits

Based on what is and is not demonstrated in the primary sources above, the main open challenges are:

Unifying multi-thousand-qubit registers with dense programmable entangling gates. Caltech/Endres show world-leading storage/imaging/transport at 6100 qubits, but do not at that scale demonstrate a two-qubit entangling gate fabric; Harvard/QuEra demonstrate fault-tolerant digital logic at hundreds of qubits but not yet at thousands. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłéturn23view0Ņłü

Maintaining high-fidelity entangling gates during aggressive mid-circuit movement and reuse. Both Atom Computing and Harvard/QuEra rely on movement for connectivity and/or entropy removal, and both identify movement-induced heating, trap-frequency beating (AOD intermodulation), and between-gate timing as fidelity-critical. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn33view0Ņłéturn29view0Ņłéturn24view2Ņłü

Scaling real-time decoding and control. Harvard/QuEra quantify shot-level and batch-level inference cost (e.g., reporting GPU inference time per shot for a batch in neural decoding) and explicitly show that desktop processing bottlenecks can dominate cycle time. ŅłĆciteŅłéturn28view5Ņłéturn34view6Ņłü This implies that future ŌĆ£large logical qubit countsŌĆØ in neutral atoms will likely require **tight hardwareŌĆōsoftware co-design**: FPGA/GPU/ASIC decoding, deterministic low-latency control loops, and fault-tolerant protocol scheduling.

Power/optics engineering for giant arrays. EndresŌĆÖs 6100-qubit scaling depends on very high optical power and a large numerical-aperture, large-FOV objective, while PasqalŌĆÖs cryogenic work underscores the thermal-load and optical-access challenges when scaling trap count (especially under cryogenic constraints). ŅłĆciteŅłéturn17view0Ņłéturn15view0Ņłü
